freestiler 0.1.0
  • Reference
    • Getting Started
    • MapLibre Tiles (MLT)
    • Python Setup

MapLibre Tiles (MLT)

Source: vignettes/maplibre-tiles.qmd

What are vector tiles?

Vector tiles divide geographic data into a grid of square tiles at multiple zoom levels. Instead of pre-rendering images (raster tiles), they store the actual geometry and attribute data, allowing client-side styling, interaction, and dynamic rendering. The standard container for vector tilesets is PMTiles – a single-file, cloud-native archive that can be served from any static file host.

MVT: the incumbent format

Mapbox Vector Tiles (MVT) is the dominant vector tile encoding. Introduced by Mapbox in 2014, MVT uses Protocol Buffers (protobuf) to serialize geometry and attributes. It is supported by every major mapping library: MapLibre GL, Mapbox GL, Leaflet, deck.gl, and more.

MVT encodes geometry as integer coordinates in a 4096x4096 pixel grid using relative move/line commands. Attributes are stored per-feature using a key-value tag system with string interning.

MLT: the next generation

MapLibre Tiles (MLT) is a columnar binary tile format announced in January 2026 by the MapLibre organization. Where MVT stores data row-by-row (one feature at a time), MLT organizes data column-by-column – all x-coordinates together, all y-coordinates together, all values of a given attribute together. This columnar layout enables efficient compression techniques:

  • Varint encoding – small integers use fewer bytes
  • Delta encoding – coordinates stored as differences from previous values
  • Run-length encoding (RLE) – repeated values compressed to count + value
  • Dictionary encoding – string columns with few unique values stored as indices into a dictionary

Size comparison

For polygon-heavy datasets, MLT is typically 10-20% smaller than MVT:

Dataset MVT MLT Savings
NC counties (z0-14) 78 KB 65 KB 17%
US block groups (z4-12) 310 MB 259 MB 16%
Education dots (z0-12) 238 MB 237 MB ~0%

Point-only datasets see minimal savings because the coordinate data dominates and compresses similarly in both formats.

How freestiler encodes MLT

freestiler includes a spec-compliant MLT encoder written in Rust. The encoding pipeline for each tile:

  1. Feature sorting – features are sorted by Morton curve (Z-order) within each tile for optimal delta encoding of coordinates.

  2. Geometry streams – coordinates are delta-encoded and varint-compressed. Topology (ring lengths, part counts) uses integer RLE when beneficial.

  3. Property streams – each attribute column is encoded independently:

    • Integers: varint + delta encoding
    • Floats: raw IEEE 754
    • Strings: dictionary encoding when cardinality is low, raw otherwise
    • Booleans: byte-RLE with ORC-style present streams

  4. Nullable handling – null/NA values produce PRESENT bitmap streams using byte-RLE encoding, matching the ORC specification.

The encoder has been validated against the mlt-core 0.1.2 reference decoder.

The freestiler pipeline 

sf / GeoDataFrame / file / SQL query
    │
    ▼
R or Python: decompose geometries + extract properties
    │
    ▼
Rust: assign features to tiles (buffered bounds)
    │
    ▼
Rust: per-tile (parallel via rayon)
    ├── clip geometry to tile boundary
    ├── snap to pixel grid (simplification)
    ├── encode as MLT or MVT
    └── gzip compress
    │
    ▼
Rust: assemble PMTiles v3 archive
    │
    ▼
.pmtiles file

Key performance features:

  • Two-level parallelism – rayon processes tiles in parallel AND features within tiles in parallel.
  • Tile-pixel grid snapping – instead of Douglas-Peucker simplification, coordinates snap to the tile’s 4096x4096 pixel grid. Shared vertices between adjacent polygons snap to the same pixel, preventing slivers.
  • Buffered assignment – features are assigned to tiles using expanded bounds (5% buffer) matching the clipping buffer. This eliminates seam artifacts at tile boundaries.
  • Sub-pixel fast paths – features smaller than one pixel at a given zoom level are skipped, dramatically reducing work at low zooms.

MLT ecosystem status

MLT is a new format (January 2026). Current ecosystem support:

  • Encoding: freestiler (Rust, R + Python), mlt-core reference encoder (Java/C++)
  • Decoding: MapLibre GL JS (experimental), mlt-core decoder
  • Viewing: MapLibre GL JS with MLT plugin

When maximum compatibility is needed today, use tile_format = "mvt". As MLT support expands across mapping libraries, the default MLT format will become directly viewable without conversion.

On this page
